intro:
Jacob (Hebrew Ya'aqob, "he grasps the heel" or "he supplants") was the younger of Isaac and Rebekah's twin sons and the third of the patriarchs, the immediate father of the twelve tribes and the man whose new name, Israel, the covenant nation would forever bear. His life — a long story of struggle, deception, exile, and transformation — traces how God's electing grace works through, and ultimately overcomes, a deeply flawed man.

Even in the womb the twins struggled, and Jacob was born grasping Esau's heel (Genesis 25:26), an omen of a life spent contending for primacy. He bought Esau's birthright for a pot of stew (Genesis 25:29–34) and, prompted by Rebekah, stole the blessing of the firstborn by deceiving his blind father Isaac (Genesis 27:19). Fleeing Esau's wrath toward Haran, he met God at Bethel, where in a dream he saw a ladder reaching to heaven and received the Abrahamic promise of land, offspring, and blessing for all nations (Genesis 28:13–15). In Haran the deceiver was himself deceived: Laban substituted Leah for Rachel, and Jacob served twenty years, gaining two wives, two maidservants, eleven sons and a daughter, and great flocks (Genesis 29:25).

The decisive turn came as Jacob returned to face Esau. Alone at the ford of the Jabbok, he wrestled till daybreak with a mysterious man — God himself — and would not let go without a blessing. There his name was changed: "Your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel, for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed" (Genesis 32:28). He limped away from Peniel a changed man, and was reconciled with Esau (Genesis 33:4). God reaffirmed the name and the covenant at Bethel (Genesis 35:10).

Jacob's later years were shadowed by grief over the supposed death of Joseph, then crowned by reunion when Joseph, risen to power in Egypt, brought the family there to survive the famine. Jacob blessed Pharaoh (Genesis 47:10), crossed his hands to bless Ephraim above Manasseh, and on his deathbed pronounced prophetic blessings over his twelve sons — including the great oracle of the scepter not departing from Judah (Genesis 49:10). He died in Egypt and was carried back to be buried with Abraham and Isaac in the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 49:29–31). Theologically, Jacob is the supreme biblical case study in sovereign grace: Paul cites "Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated," chosen before either had done good or evil, to show that God's purpose of election rests not on works but on His call (Romans 9:11–13). The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Exodus 3:6) is forever named by this man whom grace remade.
